topic: A student finds out their scholarship was meant for someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp parchment felt like sandpaper under my fingertips as I unfolded the official notification. My breath hitched, a sharp, involuntary sound in the quiet library corner. The words swam before my eyes: "Scholarship awarded to Elias Vance." Elias Vance. I stared at the name, a slow, deliberate tightening in my chest. It wasn't just disappointing; it was a deliberate slight. I remembered the hours I’d poured into my application, the sleepless nights fueled by ambition, all for this hollow realization. A cold knot formed in my stomach, hardening into something sharp and unforgiving. I crumpled the paper, the sound loud in the silence, and shoved it deep into my backpack, the fabric protesting under the force. I had to find a way to make sure Elias Vance understood the cost of such misplaced generosity.

[story 2]

"Of course," I muttered, the sound barely audible above the hum of the fluorescent lights in the administrative office. I leaned back in my chair, crossing my arms tightly over my chest, trying to keep my jaw from clenching so hard it ached. The confirmation email was undeniable: the funding, the prestige, the entire pathway I’d meticulously planned—it belonged to someone else. A slow, almost imperceptible smirk touched the corner of my mouth, quickly suppressed. I picked up my pen, the plastic cool against my skin, and began to draft a carefully worded, impeccably polite letter. Every sentence was calibrated to be condescendingly gracious, a performance designed to highlight the absurdity of the situation for the recipient. I wanted them to feel the weight of their undeserved fortune.

[story 3]

I paced the length of my dorm room floor, the worn carpet muffling the frantic rhythm of my footsteps. Every shadow seemed to stretch and twist, mirroring the sour feeling bubbling up inside me. The scholarship announcement was a cruel joke, a public display of favoritism that stung worse than any outright rejection. I stopped by the window, staring out at the indifferent cityscape, my knuckles white where I gripped the sill. A deep, simmering resentment began to coil in my gut, a hot, restless energy demanding an outlet. I needed to prove that my effort mattered, that this injustice would not go unaddressed. I pulled out my phone, the screen illuminating my face with a hard, determined glare. Time to start making things uncomfortable for whoever benefited from this deception.
